sections.recommendation:
Staff will return to Council by the end of 2025 with a resolution for the triannual adoption
of the existing Berkeley Building, Historical and Fire Codes that will be applicable to
Berkeley Fire Zone 3 and 4. These amendments to the 2025 California Building
Standards will strengthen defenses against ignition of structures from embers, radiant
heat and direct flame impingement during wildfire. Staff seeks Council’s input on this
proposal and plan to return to Council at the end of 2025 with a resolution proposed for
adoption that incorporates the Council’s feedback.
The amendments may include but are not limited to requiring Class A roofs during
partial replacement of non-compliant roof and roof structures, requiring replacement of
combustible siding with California Building Code (CBC) Chapter 7A compliant siding
during partial replacements, installation of metal gutters and screens, installation of
Chapter 7A compliant vents or vent screens, replacement of combustible decks and
fences with Chapter 7A compliant materials and design during partial replacement, and
six inches of vertical separation measured from the ground to any combustible siding.
Because of the fiscal impact that some of this work will have for homeowners, staff
plans to investigate Federal, State or Municipal programs that would provide incentives
and loans for improvements to existing non-compliant homes that focus on the highest
priority items, such as roof material, siding, decks, ember resistant vents, and a 6”
vertical separation between the siding and the ground at the base of a home near the
foundation.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
A considerable amount of staff time will be required to research and draft these code
amendments. There may also be additional revenue resulting from modifications to the
code that require additional or more expansive building and fire permits.

sections.current_situation:
Chapter 7A of the California Building Code sets fire-resistant construction requirements
for buildings in wildfire-prone areas, focusing on materials and designs that reduce the
risk of ignition from direct flame, radiant heat and embers.
Compliance with Chapter 7A – especially when coupled with code compliant defensible
space - has been shown to significantly increase structure survival rates and would be
necessary for a parcel to achieve full protection from direct flame, radiant heat and
embers. Chapter 7A requires construction features such as Class A non-combustible
roof assemblies, double-pane windows, ember-resistant vents, gutter guards, non-
combustible exterior walls, under-floor protection such as gypsum sheathing, and decks
made from approved non-combustible material. Retroactive enforcement of Chapter 7A
of the Building Code would be ideal but is not legally possible and would create a
substantial burden on residents. It is important to note that Chapter 7A is designed to
work in concert with, and not as a substitute for, defensible space recommendations
proposed within the EMBER initiative.
The State already mandates that new homes built in a Very High Fire Hazard Severity
Zone meet the requirements of Chapter 7A. However, only 50 homes in Berkeley’s Very
High Fire Hazard Severity Zone, and around 10 in the proposed Fire Zone 4, were built
since these code changes went into effect in 2008. Given this reality, the challenge to
the community is how to “harden” the ignition prone, pre-2008 constructed homes so
there is a contiguous buffer of homes that will resist ignition as a neighborhood.
Further, some structures in the Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone still have wood
shake roofs. Under the current code, homeowners are permitted to repair up to 50% of
a wood shake roof (over a five-year period). In the 1991 Oakland-Berkeley Hills Fire, it
was estimated that each burning home with a wood shake roof contributed to the
ignition of ten other homes1. Thi
sections.rationale:
The fire and seismic danger and other local conditions justify the research and potential
local amendments that are stricter than the California Building Code.
CONSIDERATIONS FOR COUNCIL
We live in a fire dependent landscape and it is inevitable that a wildfire will impact the
city again.
So, the question is, if not this, then what? All the home hardening mitigation measures
described in this staff report, along with Zone 0 must be implemented on a sufficient
number of contiguous parcels in a neighborhood for the wildfire hazard to be
meaningfully reduced. Doing anything less has been validated to be almost
meaningless with regard of hazard reduction.
If we are unwilling or unable to move forward with EMBER, then the best use of our
wildfire funds may be to continue to prepare to move residents out of the way of a fast-
moving wildfire and prepare for substantially greater loss and the burden of rebuilding.
CONTACT PERSON
David Sprague, Fire Chief, 510-981-3473[Phone Number]

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###-N.S.
EMBER: BUILDING CODE AMENDMENTS
WHEREAS, enacted in 2008, Chapter 7A of the California Building Code sets fire-
resistant construction requirements “home hardening” for buildings in wildfire-prone
areas, focusing on materials and designs that reduce the risk of ignition from direct
flame, radiant heat and embers, and
WHEREAS, home hardening is designed to work in concert with, and not as a substitute
for, defensible space, including Zone 0, and
WHEREAS, the State already mandates that new homes built in a Very High Fire
Hazard Severity Zone meet the requirements of Chapter 7A. However, only 50 homes
in Berkeley’s Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone, and
WHEREAS, in the 1991 Oakland-Berkeley Hills Fire, it was estimated that each burning
home with a wood shake roof contributed to the ignition of ten other homes, and
WHEREAS, the challenge to the community is how to “harden” the ignition prone, pre-
2008 constructed homes so there is a contiguous buffer of homes that will resist ignition
as a neighborhood.
NOW THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED that the Council of the City of Berkeley adopts
a resolution directing the City Manager to research and propose amendments to the
existing Berkeley Building, Historical and Fire Codes in the next triannual code adoption
process adopting amendments to the 2025 California Building Standards that will
strengthen defenses against ignition of structures from embers, radiant heat and direct
flame impingement during a wildfire.
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that these amendments may include but are not limited to
requiring Class A roofs during partial replacement of non-compliant roof and roof
structures, requiring replacement of combustible siding with California Building Code
(CBC) Chapter 7A compliant siding during partial replace
